comments.title: A New Year's Symbel Boast and Gods as the Soul of Other Gods
comments.content:
Good Evening,

A very happy New Year to everyone here! Whatever may be going on in the world, may you find some happiness in your own corner of it.

To Share: For the past two years, I've made a boast of what I plan to do for the coming year, and I'm continuing that for this coming year. If anyone feels so inclined, you may join me in a virtual symbel by raising a toast to what I hope to accomplish and adding anything you have done in the past year, or want to be darn sure you get done in the coming year. Fair warning: this is an oath before Gods and men, so don't join in unless you really mean it! I explain a bit more in my latest post: https://jpowellrussell.com/#a_boast_for_2024

But for ease, here's the boast itself:

Hail Gods
Great and mighty,
welcome world-crafters!
Ale I lift
to Ese* shining,
beloved life-shapers.

Hail Forebears
Far-spread about,
and full-told through time!
To you I toast
with tumbler high,
wellsprings of widespread kin.

May coming year
kindly yield
blissful blessings to all
Boldly will I
a book full-write
on prayers of fair praise.
* Ese is a proposed Anglo-Saxon version of Aesir, the chief tribe of the Gods, and which can be used to refer to all Gods, even the Wen/Vanir

To Ask: In The Weird of Hali series, you refer to Nyarlathotep a few times as the "soul and mighty messenger of the Great Old Ones." With full consideration given for the fact that this is from a series of fantasy novels, and might just have been a nice bit of imagery, I've encountered the idea that a God(dess) is the "soul" of a group of Gods before (Maria Kvilhaug argues that Idhunn plays that role for the Norse Gods), and I'm still very confused what that might mean. Other than a great theme for meditation and/or poetic license, what would it mean for an individualized, independently acting conscious being to be the soul of a group of other conscious beings?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: A New Year's Symbel Boast and Gods as the Soul of Other Gods
replies.content: That's a fine question, but the person who could best answer it is H.P. Lovecraft -- he's the one who defined Nylarlathotep in those terms. I have no idea what he meant by it, but since it's canon in the Cthulhu mythos I used the label in my books.

replies.content:
Never take Lovecraft too seriously. He’s classified as a horror writer but it’s pretty clear that he and his friends were mostly having fun. When he invented Nyarlathotep, he may well have been thinking something like “Messenger of the gods...yeah, that sounds good...let’s see if Bob Bloch comes up with a bad pun and a good story.”

I exempt “The Color Out Of Space,” about radiation poisoning, from the above. That one’s scary as hell (literally). Lovecraft was on record as saying much of his material was inspired by his dreams. If he dreamed up that one, he has my respect for not being afraid to go back to bed.

It’s a shame life is so Puritanically grim these days. The world needs a new Lovecraft Circle. Let’s all hope that JMG’s tentacular fun inspired someone else to do more of same.

—Princess Cutekitten

comments.title: HNY!!
comments.content:
Happy new year to everyone! I hope 2024 is personally gratifying for everyone even if the wider world has a lot of moving pieces.

Had a few questions for JMG and the commentariat.

1) There's a few people I talk to who have a cosmology which seems similar to the Mithiraic cosmology (both iranian and in the roman era), or Philip K Dick's urgrund, or the neoplatonic strain that influenced gnosticism.

They seem to believe in a larger hypercosmic god who is not active (yet) within our world, and our world is the protectorate of a lower level entity that isn't evil but is blind in some way. They also seem skeptical of reincarnation (as in, they think its real but is some sort of control structure of the lower sphere entities, and not important to our progress).

They believe the abrahamic religions all worship the lower entity, while plato, the egyptians, iranians, chaldeans (maybe even the old hindus) etc all correctly identify the cosmology of the universe and worship the higher entity. Mithiras for example has influence during precession of the equinoxes (though I need to learn more about what this actually means).

I know you've indicated in that past that the solar logos of our solar system is sort of the tiphareth entity of our system, equivalent to christ etc. Would that mean their cosmology is accurate except this solar logos and other lower entities isn't bad/evil as they seem to think?

2) Would you be able to point to any readings/writers to make sense of these topics?

2) They said the hypercosmic god in mithiraic legends as well as neoplatonism seems to relate to freemasonry and other "brotherhood of humankind" movements through history. There is a case being made that Plato's work was a noble lie to move humanity away from the mimetic and violent consciousness of Homeric Greek into a self-aware active concept of freedom, knowledge and behavior.

If that's the case, then the relative loss in the important of freemasonry and other groups is linked to a lot of the external poltical problems we have now (in the sense that the higher cosmic god or principle doesn't exert itself as before and other lower gods are exerting themselves).

What do you think?


For anyone who wants to read a summary of PKD's views:
https://dickiangnosticism.wordpress.com/2017/11/12/cosmogony-and-cosmology/

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content:
1) This is a classic version of the anticosmic end of Gnosticism. I disagree with it for many of the same reasons Plotinus and the other Neoplatonists did -- Ennead II.9 in the writings of Plotinus, "Against the Gnostics," summarizes this well. A sample:

"There are men, bound to human bodies and subject to desire, grief, anger, who think so generously of their own faculty that they declare themselves in contact with the Intelligible World, but deny that the sun possesses a similar faculty less subject to influence, to disorder, to change; they deny that it is any wiser than we, the late born, hindered by so many cheats on the way towards truth.

"Their own soul, the soul of the least of mankind, they declare deathless, divine; but the entire heavens and the stars within the heavens have had no communion with the Immortal Principle, though these are far purer and lovelier than their own souls — yet they are not blind to the order, the shapely pattern, the discipline prevailing in the heavens, since they are the loudest in complaint of the disorder that troubles our earth."

2) Nearly all the Nag Hammadi Gnostic treatises, and the huge literature on that end of Gnosticism, are written from within that end of the Gnostic tradition. Plotinus and the Neoplatonists generally are a good intro to the other side of the argument.

3) If that were the case, doesn't that make the hypercosmic god inferior to the cosmic gods, since the former's power depends on such petty affairs as the rise and fall of human social institutions? That seems a little dubious...

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content:
Thanks for the references!

Do you think this strain of thinking is driven by fear and paranoid of the thinkers projected out to their understanding of the spiritual world?

On 3, I think it us more like our powers and development as humans depend on associating with higher principles/god rather than the lower gods who are closer to human experience and affairs generally but may be much more volatile and delusional.

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content:
1) I wouldn't go that far. For some people, the gap between the transcendent and the manifested worlds is difficult to bear, and so they tend to see the manifested world as inferior or evil, a "black iron prison" in which we are all caught. Others find the difference less intolerable and can appreciate the manifested world for what it is, even though it's imperfect and confused. These two groups of people naturally find their way to different spiritual paths.

2) Fair enough. I suspect, though, that a lot of the people who believe this have never attended a Masonic lodge meeting; as a Mason I respect the Craft greatly, but it's no hotbed of transcendent wisdom...

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content:
Mithras is one of the deities that is part of my primary focus of worship. If you want a few resources to get you started in understanding some of those issues, I recommend the following:

"The Mithraic Mysteries" by David Ulansey (addresses the precession of equinoxes): http://www.mysterium.com/sciam.html

The Tertullian Project's pages on the Roman cult of Mithras: https://www.tertullian.org/rpearse/mithras/display.php?page=main

Ceisiwr Serith's Mithraism pages: https://www.ceisiwrserith.com/mith/index.htm

And for a short but very informative modern scholarly book that tells us what we can know of the Mithraic Mysteries from the surviving evidence (hint, it ain't much), I recommend "The Roman Cult of Mithras: The God and His Mysteries" by Manfred Clauss. Clauss is a German scholar who is one of the most prominent modern scholars of Mithraism. The book is a great resource.

As a final note, I can say that Mithras is a God who will reveal himself and his will only to the extent that it is needed to accomplish that will. It is, in my experience, the case that he offers (along with Christ) a sort of salvation by gnosis. Whether that validates the overall world weary kind of Gnosticism that JMG mentions is a different question, of course.

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content: I'd gotten the impression that at least historically, Mithraists tended to fall much more to the Neoplatonist end of things, recognizing the cosmos as a valid expression of the divine rather than the antithesis of divinity. That was part of what attracted so many practical Roman men of affairs to his worship: they wanted to accomplish good in the world and He helped them do that.

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content: I agree with you on that.  The Mithraic Mysteries, from what evidence is left, seems to have a been mildly gnostic flavored take on Neoplatonism.  The world isn't an evil or bad place, though it is impermanent.  Completion of the mysteries provided some kind of gnostic experience of the God himself, and this was also salvific in some manner. Mithraism seems to have appealed to upwardly mobile men in the middle classes: soldiers, freedmen, mid-level government bureaucrats, etc.  Mithras offered, then and now, assistance in this life and in the life that follows it.  I suppose not too different from many other gods, really.  And I can attest from personal experience that his assistance in this life is really quite effective.  On two occasions he saved from me death in automobile crashes, and has helped me in many other ways.

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content:
What's been your experience of worshipping Mithras?

Do you know how he relates to the precession gates (June and Dec) in terms of his influence on earth?

replies.title: Re: HNY!!
replies.content:
My experience? Honestly, he's not too different in demeanor from Christ. He's fairly easy to approach, and on the forgiving side. If one is willing to put in the work, show him respect, and apologize for shortcomings and mistakes, then he's quite helpful in everyday life. As I mentioned above, he saved me from what would have been two likely fatal car crashes. The interesting thing is that I had only recently begun worshiping him when all this happened. I get a sense of almost military-like camaraderie behind that. A sort of, "you're a new recruit to my unit, but you're one of my men now. I'll be here for you." He certainly has for me, and in many different ways.

As for the precessions, the key to understanding his influence is in the myth of the sacrifice of the Bull. Though we don't have a surviving version of it, between the plentiful surviving iconography, some inscriptions, and a few indirect pieces of it in ancient Pagan and Christian writings, we can reconstruct the basic form and what it likely meant. In short, his sacrifice of the Bull is a cosmos renewing salvific action. The various animals that accompany Mithras during the sacrifice (as evidenced in the surviving iconography) consume the various powers of the Bull upon its death (via its blood, semen, etc.). Also, in some depictions, things like grain come forth from the Bull as Mithras sacrifices it.

In short, the precessions occur because Mithras sacrifices the Bull, renewing the cosmos and continuing it along its "movement." His influence is that of a solar deity who helps sustain the cosmic order, and to renew it as needed. I haven't given the precession of the equinoxes a whole lot of thought. In the surviving iconography, Mithras was born from a rock, and ready to complete his mission of sacrificing the Bull. The winter equinox represents a new birth of his power into the world, and the summer equinox is when it reaches its special apex, the sacrifice of the Bull and the renewing power that comes from that.

comments.title: The Solution is More Cats
comments.content:
Just wanted to share a TSW moment from an Egyptian Polytheist:

I had some drama crop up a few months ago in my otherwise Christian family. Bast approached me and asked if I could get permission to pray for the situation, and after doing so, I did a few small general prayers for a peaceable resolution before being told it was enough, so I let it sit.

Fast forward to this Christmas only to find that everyone involved in the drama has independently either gotten a cat or strongly considered getting a cat as a solution to various tangential problems that were feeding the drama. Even people who were adamantly opposed to having a cat on allergy grounds have discovered the existence of low-allergen breeds and are looking into them, and a dog owner has discovered that a cat with the with the proper attitude can in fact make a good companion animal for an adult dog where a puppy would have too much energy.

So apparently Bast is alive and active in North America and doesn't mind helping Christians out from time to time! I'm even more amazed because I never prayed for cats or mentioned them or Bast to the people I was praying for - I just did a few general private prayers for people that lived hundreds of miles away from me and suddenly cats appeared to assist the situation.

The gods of Egypt seem to be very active here in North America; when I worked with the classic Golden Dawn system I developed a good working relationship with the deities of the Ennead of Heliopolis and got some dramatic results while working with them, and I know other people who have had equally good results from invoking them.

comments.title: Self-impsed prisons
comments.content:
Wishing everyone a peaceful and spiritually interesting year if you consent to such wishes.

Thanks for this forum, JMG.

I have been practicing a protective ritual for about 18 months, started with the SOP but then moved to the Ritual of the Three Rays, with which I feel a greater affinity and that I manage to skip less often.

For about a year I have also practiced discursive meditation - 10 minutes per day.

Recently you mentioned on a podcast and on the other blog that people can voluntarily imprison themselves in a mindset that is harmful for them. I definitely do that and my prison is depression and hopelessness.

My meditation usually follows a verbal path of associations but when I decided to meditate on my prison a visual path seemed more natural. I saw myself sitting behind a barred window behind a wall but it was only one wall. So I managed to convince myself toe get up and walk around the wall. But then I was in another big chamber with one window (also barred) that could not open and no door that I could find.

The second session devoted to this did not progress much beyond the first apart from being able to break the glass in the window in the larger chamber so that I could at least feel and smell the fresh air. But I felt very stuck and could not see anywhere to go with this path. I then tried a verbal path but that did not yield anything helpful.

My question is: should I continue this theme even though I feel that I'm getting nowhere or might it be better to choose a different theme and come back to this some other time?

Thanks
Blue ballooning stick insect

replies.title: Re: Self-impsed prisons
replies.content: What I'd encourage you to do at this point is to take the imagery and explore it conceptually in meditation. It seems fairly clear to me that the prison you think you're in is not the one you're really in; depression and hopelessness is the single wall with the barred window in it. There's something else, and you use depression and hopelessness to distract yourself from it. I'm not sure if this is true for you, but for many people I know who are seriously depressed, the depression is a cover for anger, and beneath the anger is something else -- maybe shame, maybe grief, maybe fear, but something from childhood that has to be brought to the surface and dealt with so the whole structure can dissolve like mist.

replies.title: Re: Self-impsed prisons
replies.content:
I have a friend (or really an acquaintance) who has very similar layers, and I just recently described them in the same way.

I can say from my own experience that there is definitely something underneath the anger underneath the depression, and that getting to that layer ends up dissolving the depression. I would also add that my magical practice was the thing that allowed me to get to that much deeper layer.

Anyway, I wish everyone here a Happy New Year and I hope 2024 turns out to be a good year for everyone here.

Thank you JMG for this forum.

comments.title: Karma and the Gods
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have been reflecting on karma, and the gods, based off some of your recent comments on this blog and your excellent essay on it. My take, for what it's worth, is that if gods exist eternally, as in out of time, they are likely to be able to see the end result of every action as it happens, and whilst this does not exempt the gods from karma - it applies to every being as I understand it, being hard-wired into the very substance of the cosmos ('tracks in space'), if the gods can see the end result of every action instantly, then they are in a position to understand it, and organise, or be judicious in any interventions they undertake (that is, if they undertake them at all).

I can see how people get caught up into endless battles of theodicy in not understanding the crucial nature of an eternal, timeless world, when considering karma.

Secondly, it's also occurred to me that whilst thoughts technically do carry karmic consequence, most thoughts are the consequence of previous action (childhood trauma being one), so if the gods are to influence actions, they are most likely to minimise their own karma by influencing thoughts ('changing consciousness in accordance with will' - their will), rather than altering or redirecting any specific actions after they are taken, although they probably could do this if they had to.

I have also wondered that whilst the eternal world is timeless, it is not limitless, so even for those gods, there are limits that they cannot span, or maybe could span with movement or change, and beyond that still, is the great unmanifest, of which not a lot can really be known.

Would this be a valid take?

replies.title: Re: Karma and the Gods
replies.content: It certainly seems valid to me.

comments.title: Loki
comments.content:
Happy New Year to JMG and the commentariat!

Not so much a question, but a mildly amusing story to start the new year. (Apologies if this has been mentioned before though - I've not been on in a few weeks) It relates to film and tv though, so you may not have heard about it.

Marvel Studios, who make the Iron Man and Avengers movies, were purchased by Disney a few years ago, and they have since been trying to milk the franchise for all it's worth.

As such, there is a spin-off series based on the character Loki, who also appears as a villain-turned-hero in the films.

Even at the time the first series came on, I thought "This had better be a decent portrayal, or a certain god is not going to be happy".

The Loki of the films stayed pretty close to both the comic books and the mythological character, causing general destruction and mayhem in a bid for power and chaos. The Loki of the series (particularly the second series)? A sad, confused, desperate and frightened character who causes zero mischief and relies on supporting characters to tell him what he's supposed to be doing, despite technically obtaining power over all space and time.

If I were a god of mischief, and was portrayed as such, I'd be kind of annoyed. And of all the gods to annoy, mischievous ones are probably second only to Saturnian ones in terms of bad ideas.

Anyway, a couple of weeks ago, Disney reluctantly announced they were dropping a major actor from all their subsequent films due to a domestic abuse lawsuit (though note I don't endorse this, nor am I making light of it).

Now the villain character in question was the villain of the Loki series, and was scheduled to be the main villain in the next 5 movies, as well as further spin-offs. Most other characters could be written-out or their faces/identities changed, but not this one - the whole thing is that there are millions of him coming from across all timelines, who all look the same. Even in the series, you can see that Disney clearly ordered thousands of props with his face on them (eg. 10 foot statues, whole walls), and have presumably ordered far more for the films already, as well as all the associated merchandise. Now I'm not *saying* that this is the vengeance of a god, just that *if* I were a god of mischief (and I'm quite a mischievous person), bringing someone's purported wrongdoings to light in a way that financially cripples the company that slighted me is the sort of thing I would do.

Unofficially, Marvel Studios is apparently now in pandemonium trying to rewrite the scripts, which will, at the very least, cause significant delays to their main cash-cow films. What with the enforced wokery and very public disputes with the state of Florida, Disney is not doing well, though I doubt they will learn from it or change direction.

Meanwhile, I can just imagine Loki laughing himself silly chained to his rock.

And on a side note - why do so many dog owners name their pets Loki?? Do people just think "Man, I really need some more chaos and disorder in my life". I keep encountering Loki-dogs of all breeds whilst walking my own dog and they are invariably badly behaved and disobedient creatures (though presumably still quite lovable).

Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Loki
replies.content:
It's a nasty bind.

They can't leave him in, because then they might be seen as supporting domestic violence.

They can't take him out without eating significant sunk costs. Besides the obvious downside of delayed revenue with respect to shareholders, the longer you delay for something like this, the greater percentage of your fanbase you are going to lose to something else.

They can't even use CGI or similar, because it's still his face and voice (and then you also get into potential Screen Actors Guild issues).

replies.title: Re: Loki
replies.content: Disney has clearly offended the gods, which is why they can't stop making movies that lose money. "Whom the gods would destroy, they first make mad" -- or stupid, as the case may be. I'll look forward to their messy corporate bankruptcy.

replies.title: Re: Loki
replies.content: For what it's worth, "bringing someone's purported wrongdoings to light" isn't the only factor that could be manipulated to produce that outcome. If you knew "in advance" that a producer was bound and determined to depict you in a way you didn't like, you could start arranging for someone about whom that kind of wrongdoing would be purportable to be cast in that kind of crucial role. You could also arrange for whatever role they were "already going to be" in to become more crucial than it otherwise would have been. (And those are all from among just the conventionally deific options.) Of course, this is a more imprecise way of speaking about someone's range of possible actions than in usual speech, because the language's baked-in assumptions about time, prediction, possibility, and distinctness are more off than usual.

comments.title: Picatrix ritual
comments.content:
A very happy new year to everybody, and, if you want, my blessings for your year - may it bring you whatever you desire!

I‘ve been pondering the Perfect Nature ritual in Picatrix, book 3, chapter 6. That chapter contains a lot of meditation fodder, but I‘ve also got some practical questions:

1. In footnote 212, you write that this chapter „seems to bridge the gap“ between classical (Graeco-Roman) rituals and early modern rituals for evoking the guardian spirit.

I‘m not sure what you meant by „it bridges the gap“: That the classical and the early modern rituals of that kind are essentially the same in structure, type, etc, and that this ritual helped conserve the tradition? Or that this ritual is something like an intermediate point between the two different stages (classical and early modern)?

2. Is there any literature in general which you could recommend about how occult rituals in the Western tradition evolved over time?

3. There is a very specific instruction in this ritual to use pitchers with a capacity of about a pint, and I‘m not sure if this is just more meditation fodder, or if there is a specific reason for that amount? Does this e.g. have any practical background which I‘m missing?

A pint of sesame or almond oil would have been a lot back then - probably unaffordable for a lot of people.

4. Would you happen to know if anybody has tried this ritual, and with what results?

I‘ve been wondering if rituals of a certain kind work best in a specific era, maybe because the energies of that era are conducive to a certain type of ritual, or because people are more attuned and open to certain kinds of energies? Or is this a matter of what works, works, no matter when and where?

5. Finally, one would think that a writer doesn‘t have to advertise a ritual like this with additional advantages besides the main goal, but of course, like all good rituals, this one will also give the practitioner increased business, more possessions and every other desirable thing under the sun (some of which, like the possessions, seem to be in direct contrast to the main goal, but hey - these writers sure knew human nature…). ;-)

But why did none of the people who wrote up those rituals have the common sense to advertise them with more practical achievements, such as „your bathroom will forever be clean, and all your dishes will keep doing themselves, in this life and all your future lives“?? I keep thinking that a ritual which is advertised with such benefits would „sell“ like hotcakes… ;-)

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Picatrix ritual
replies.content:
1) The gap in question is purely historical -- you have late classical rituals of the type, and then you have early modern rituals of the same type. The Picatrix falls in between and shows that the same practices were going on all the while.

2) I wish there was.

3) I've wondered about that myself. It seems a lot to use up in a meal!

4) I know of people who've done it with good results, but I haven't heard detailed reports.

5) Because they're not interested in advertising. The Picatrix and the older occult traditions in general have zero interest in attracting the masses -- magic in these traditions is meant for the few.

replies.title: Re: Picatrix ritual
replies.content:
Thanks!

3) There's that, too. It does mention feasting together with friends (a group ritual?? or would one only invite one's sage friends over afterwards?), but still... eating up more than 4 pints of fat in one meal would require a sizeable number of friends (or group ritual participants).

I suppose one would be allowed to eat something like bread with it, wouldn't one?

The inevitable leftoevers... pouring them on the ground as sacrifice to the gods?

This is a really weird part of the instruction. If whoever did this ritual ate it all up without getting sick, I'm duly impressed. ;-)

Milkyway

comments.title: Esoteric Spirituality
comments.content:
Happy new year, JMG and forumistas-- may blessings abound!

Last week I read The Western Esoteric Traditions by Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke. He lists with approval Antoine Faivre's six "fundamental characteristics of esoteric spirituality." To paraphrase:
  1. "Correspondences": There are sympathies and connections that symbolically link everything in the universe.
  2. "Living Nature": There is an indivisible energy or soul animating the universe.
  3. "Imagination and Mediations": There is a supra-sensory realm 'between' the material and divine, populated by spiritual intelligences.
  4. "The Experience of Transmutation": The subject is transformed by following an esoteric system.
  5. "Concordance": Esoteric traditions postulate an underlying or ancestral unity among their systems.
  6. "Transmission": Initiation is a common mode of instruction within a system.
Something about this formulation just 'clicked' for me and I feel a new clarity about esotericism. JMG, is there anything you would add? Do you see any reason to be cautious about the list?

comments.title: Xenoglossy and Glossolalia
comments.content:
I have been having an extended discussion/conversation concerning the "hard problem". A hypothesis has been proposed that there is actually a communication between the observed and the observer and that communication, solely in the mind of the observer, is what constitutes reality.

But it has been posited that any communication de facto implies a language. Since this is hotly discussed in the ongoing conversation, I proposed that Glossolalia and Xenoglossy are an ability to express this language directly. I am not advocating this idea, I am just exploring the possibility.

Anyone's thought and opinions on this would be greatly appreciated.

There is a reason that this is referred to as "the hard problem".

replies.title: Re: Xenoglossy and Glossolalia
replies.content:
It depends on what you mean by "language." If the word refers to any channel or means whatever of communicating between two or more beings, then the answer is trivially a "YES." Communication is communicating.

If, however, you mean a system of communication using words (which could be all listed in a dictionary) to construct sentences according to some system of rules (which could be described in a grammar), then the answer is a resounding "NO"!

There are all sorts of ways people convey things to one another that use no words or sentences: the taste of a favorite food cooked by a friend, the scents, smells and pheromones that signal emotions, the significant glances that call to mind shared memories, the degree of closeness between two people standing or sitting together, the narrowed gaze and set jaw of agression, and so forth. And as between people, so between any observed and observer.

As for glossolalia, take a look at William Samarin's Tongues of Men and Angels (1972). Samarin is an anthropologist working chiefly with African cultures, but he also was born and raised in a Russian sectarian émigré community (called the Molokane in Russian) in Canada where speaking in tongues was a routine part of the commnunity's religious life. So he is no outsider to the phemonemon.

See also Tanya Luhrmann's When God Talks Back (2012) and How God Becomes Real (2020). She is a prominent anthropologist who has ben looking for years at the means by which people make their Gods become real to them, as real as any human person, or even more so. (Yes, this is the same Tanya Luhrmann who wrote Persuasions of the Witche's Craft way back in 1989.)

replies.title: Re: Xenoglossy and Glossolalia
replies.content:
Bentley Little wrote a pretty good horror novel, The Town, about Molokans battling The Forces Of Evil. He was raised Molokan, and I enjoyed his take on pacifists struggling to reconcile their belief with life in a non-pacifist world. He really draws you into their dilemma.

—Princess Cutekitten

comments.title: theosophy and reincarnation
comments.content:
Greetings JMG and Happy secular new year:

Many years ago, I ran across a series of books by Anthony Borgia which claimed to be what we would now call "channeled" from a Monsignor Robert Hugh Benson. These books gave a view of the afterlife quite different from the Christian Heaven and Hell, one in which people of ordinary spiritual level went to what I can only term the English countryside, with cottages, lakes to swim in, outdoor concert halls featuring colored lights in the air above the orchestra, workshops for crafts and libraries in which the books were true. Sounded terrific. From this level they could advance to a more spiritual realm (looking back I detect a bit of Dante's Paradise as inspiration). People whose earthly lives had been less satisfactory were to be found in a series of dank, dreary tunnels. They too could advance. The premise of the books was that Benson was being allowed to visit levels other than the one to which he had been assigned so that he could inform people that the Christian view of life and death was mistaken, and that Borgia had been chosen to receive and publish these messages. I later discovered that these books had been published by the Theosophical Press.

I just discovered today that Benson had been an actual person; an Anglican priest who converted to Roman Catholicism and eventually became a member of the Pope's staff. Borgia was a medium who apparently knew Benson in life.

For the curious the titles are: _Here and Hereafter_ (don't confuse with contemporary book of same title by different author); _Life in the World Unseen_; _More About the World Unseen-. These have been reprinted by several different presses at various times, but I believe they were originally published in the 1920s or 30s. When I read them originally, in 1961, I was only 12 and paid no attention to publishers or dates of publication. I had also never heard of Theosophy. Years later in the 1990s I heard a discussion on late night radio, recognized some of the details and thought "those are the books I read years ago!" I scribbled down the author's name and located copies in the university library and reread them.

So, my question is, since Theosophy teaches reincarnation, do you think that the material in these books meant to represent the experience between incarnations? Or perhaps the press issued them on some principle of giving a voice to different points of view on the afterlife than that of Christianity even if those views did not match the teachings of Theosophy? or the teachings about the afterlife in Western occult tradition that you have written about elsewhere? Just interested in your thoughts on the matter. Thanks for your time.

Rita

replies.title: Re: theosophy and reincarnation
replies.content: Hmm! That's fascinating that the Theosophical Press would publish that; back in the day they were very sharply opposed to mediumship. The vision of the afterlife in those books is the classic Spiritualist account, for what it's worth; the term "Summerland" was much used for the afterlife in Spiritualist circles a century ago, and might be worth following up if you want to learn more.

replies.title: Re: theosophy and reincarnation
replies.content:
Benson ,and his brother R. H., were also prolific weird fiction writers. (I mean their fiction was “weird,” like the Lovecraft crowd, not that they were.)

E.F. seems to be most frequently reprinted. Some of his better-known works:

—“The Man Who Went Too Far”
—“The Room In The Tower”
—“Caterpillars”
—“How Fear Departed From The Long Gallery “
—“Negotiam Perambulans”
—“Mrs. Amworth”

There was yet a third brother, A.C., who also wrote, mostly about his own experiences as a college professor. Lovecraft was a big fan of E. F., especially of “The Man Who Went Too Far.”

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: theosophy and reincarnation
replies.content: Ah, he's that Benson! I'm sorry to hear his afterlife was so bland, then. It would have been so colorful if it had been stocked with tentacled horrors. ;-)

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1) I wanted to ask you about the discrete planes and the nature of our experience. You've said that each plane (material, etheric, etc) is discrete, and we have 3 bodies, and developing the 4th (mental).

So would it be fair to say that when people use spiritual etc they mostly mean higher astral as the source/mechanism of experience of gods, spirits, various parapsychological phenomena etc? That we don't actually have access to the higher planes (in so far as they exist)?

2) How do these planes interact with each other if the principle of sufficient reason dictates that matter and consciousness cant affect each other directly (usually people try to solve this by making all mind matter or all matter mind)? We clearly see from life that our mind affects our material lives, things like affirmations actually work etc.

Occult theory generally feels very dualist (technically quad-dualist??) which a mechanism for each plane to interact?

Making the will the underlying substance and each plane a gradient of it is some type of dual aspect theory for monism but it still relies on this metaphysical substance, "the will", we have no direct access to as the explanatory vehicle, and leaves no space for free will because matter as currently construed is a closed system.

3) I had a weird divination (which I think may be wrong just cos its so surprising) that suggested that the answer might lie in some version of interactionist dualism (blending in quantum mechanics in some way), and that dual aspect theory, panpsychism, monism was actually wrong. I'm looking into this now.

If people have thoughts and ideas, please do share.

Here's the stanford entry: https://iep.utm.edu/hard-problem-of-conciousness/#SH3e

"Interactionist dualism, of both the substance and property type, holds that consciousness is causally efficacious in the production of bodily behavior. This is certainly a strongly intuitive position to take with regard to mental causation, but it requires rejecting the causal closure of the physical. It is widely thought that the principle of causal closure is central to modern science, on par with basic conservation principles like the conservation of energy or matter in a physical reaction (see, for example, Kim 1998). And at macroscopic scales, the principle appears well-supported by empirical evidence. However, at the quantum level it is more plausible to question causal closure. On one reading of quantum mechanics, the progression of quantum-level events unfolds in a deterministic progression until an observation occurs. At that point, some views hold that the progression of events becomes indeterminstic. If so, there may be room for consciousness to influence how such “decoherence” occurs—that is, how the quantum “wave function” collapses into the classical, observable macroscopic world we experience."

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) We don't have direct access to planes above the mental, and most people have only very limited direct access to the mental plane. That's less of a problem than it may seem, however, since beings of the higher planes also have more than one body, and it's quite common for them to have astral bodies.

2) It's exactly because there are multiple planes that things can pass from mind to matter and vice versa; each plane affects the ones immediately adjacent to it, and can thus serve as intermediaries, provided that points of contact exist. The planes are discrete and not continuous, but they do intersect in complex ways. Occultism, by the way, rejects the idea of the causal closure of the physical.

3) Occult philosophy is a modified monism; all the substances of the different planes are aspects of one Cosmic root substance, but the different aspects are distinct and do not flow together. Think of the way different electromagnetic wavelengths have very different powers and effects, and pass through each other without any mutual effect -- but then you get situations where radio waves cause heat or gamma rays cause the emission of light.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Ok so occultism actually folds interactional dualism (that mind affects matter directly) into monism (one underlying substance) and is a distinct thing from panpsychism or dual aspect theory which hold that mind and matter do not interact, or mind is primitive.

This would make a bit of sense instead of trying to force fit mind into matter and matter into mind, or explain them as correlations, as is usually attempted. It also would allow things like stones to only have physical bodies and no "mind" so to speak.

This is generally my intuition too and it preserves free will. It does reject Schopenhauer bit though, since I think he accepts the causal closure of the physical / no free will within representation?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: Yes, it challenges some of Schopenhauer's ideas. I see him as a pioneer, not as the last word!

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) Do you see free will as some sort of primitive chosen by the divine to be explored in the immanent world?

2) Would you consider parallel universes to be accurate? (It would be the same from the perspective of eternity as multiple lives for a soul)

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) No, it's something that souls attain -- we're not created with it, nor are we born with it in any given life. Most human beings have little more than a bit of wiggle room, but those who develop it gradually gain the freedom it brings with them.

2) I've never encountered any reason to think that it's correct.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Really answering your question would require saying a bunch of contentious stuff about the many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics, and it's too late in the night for that. But here:

1) You should probably say "paranormal" there: "parapsychological" feels a little upside down, since it refers more to the methodology of inquiry than to the domain of phenomena.

3) It is still possible that something "outside" of quantum physics decides which timelines get experienced, which would be one kind of abrogation of the partial causal closure you'd get from a many-worlds universe, but I think most of the ways people imagine "selecting which timelines get experienced" wouldn't really interface very well with the character of quantum mechanics. (The most obvious ways to do it cause unrelated superpositions to fail.) It would be more consistent with cosmology as I understand it if the way such selection worked was more like, causing some timelines to get experienced more than they otherwise would have been, and others less.

I would link you something related I posted in response to someone's question about eight weeks ago about what principle astrology would work on -- a huge tangential speculation about the interface between the supernatural and quantum mechanics via the Wolfram physics paradigm -- but I didn't leave myself enough time to find it.

Some years ago I experienced something which may have been the birthing of one of my energetic bodies, but I'm not very confident in that answer given that I know very little about the birthing process for them. What ages are they likely to happen around, and how can you tell the differences between them?


Thanks muchly,
Lavender Ponderous Raccoon

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The etheric body is born around age 7. That's the point at which you can thrive and be healthy without regular physical contact with a parent or close caregiver. After that you have your own life energy and will begin showing the habits of health and illness you'll have for the rest of your life.

The astral body is born around age 14, or whenever you hit puberty. That one's hard to miss, since that's when you start having your own opinions and your own images of how you want to live, who you want to marry (if anyone), and so on.

The mental sheath is born around age 21, which is why that's the age of legal maturity. It's harder to typify because the effects you get depend on how developed your mental sheath is. Generally it's the point at which a personal sense of meaning, purpose, and value begins to appear -- to whatever extent that you're capable of achieving such a sense.

What happens after that? It depends on how you spend your life. If your mental sheath is well developed you may go through a series of developmental stages in which proto-organs or even fully developed mental organs become active, leading to a deepening of your mental plane function.

replies.title: Re: What is Love among Hashimoto's?
replies.content:
A dear friend has Hashimoto's, so I've learned about it. It is not an inevitable sentence of decline to awful death. Nor is it inevitable that all her offspring would have it, or that they'd suffer greatly even if they did. It is a pregnancy risk factor, but one that can be sensibly evaluated.

I encourage you to talk to the woman. Tell her the reasons you are so drawn to her. Ask her to explain her prognosis and treatment plan. Tell her honestly that you are afraid that if the relationship continues, there would be children with health problems.

I believe you are responding more to unrelated health issues with other people and to your own speculative fears and worries, than to actual medical information. Even if it did turn out to be inadvisable for you and this woman to have children together - something far from proven so far - it might still be a profoundly wonderful, harmoniously compatible loving friendship you don't have to throw away.

Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: What is Love?
replies.content:
"Perfect romantic match" doesn't necessarily mean ideal permanent partner. The semantics of your question are a little wonky already, since you were asking "perfect" to even mean anything in a world such as this, and that means you're automatically accepting that "perfect" means "perfect given some constraints". That's because of Gricean implicature: otherwise you'd be asking "will this one-in-a-googol unlikely event happen", and how would that be a reasonable interpretation of someone who hadn't thought over their words carefully, given how unlikely it is that they would have a reason to seriously communicate that question? And, since "literally perfect" is therefore off the table, at that point it's just a question of how many constraints your question implicitly includes.

I don't know how the intelligent phenomenon behind divination in general, and/or the more particular details it has in connection with geomancy, "thinks" about what it means to give a correct reply in this kind of case, but that's a major consideration that I would expect it to have "noticed".

So I would read "perfect" in your question as, "among the best I am likely to get during the relevant vaguely sized window of a few future years, with a small bias towards 'no' for partners who would genuinely be my best option going forward but only because the dice had rolled unfavorably so that getting any more boxes checked at the time wouldn't be an option and it genuinely wasn't worth waiting."

You also used the word "romantic match", rather than "long-term partnership match", or for that matter "breeding partner match". Romance is a specific thing, a specific dynamic, and it can be "perfect" in the only relevant limited sense and still be finite (and not just because of your life being finite). And the other kinds of match have other meanings with their own problems: sometimes the best trajectory available would go through a temporary relationship, or a non-breeding one. If you want to be oriented to when to focus on having your hopes up and when to focus on coping with despair, or if you want to be oriented to what opportunities are best to jump on for the sake of some specified longer-term goal and what opportunities are better to pass up, then you'll need to find other question wordings that precisely name those other uncertainties.

Perhaps the most important thing is to give up on a conception of "perfection" that is unnatural and inapposite to worlds with internally consistent mechanics and details, and transition at least to a conception of "perfection" that is compatible with the other constraints of what kind of world this is. A triangle with the perfectly maximal number of sides still only has three sides, and even a chess match where one side plays every move perfectly still isn't a mate in one.

Don't wait up for your anima-syzygy, that I assume was pushing that idea of "perfection" on you to begin with, to figure out that it needs to make that transition for you. I don't think that's its job.

YMMV, but in the cases I'm familiar with, semi-ideal matches like that seem to happen partly to give someone what they want, and partly to prod someone to accept that in the long run they are going to need to make their wants be better organized and coherent and make more sense. Though usually the person discovers this during the relationship itself, as the relationship has the natural consequences a relationship like that would realistically have.

In this case, one of your wants (mostly orthogonal to romance) was some kind of identity commitment around the medical establishment. There's some small chance that that was correct, but for the most part I would say: dealing with the institutional disaster of the medical establishment wouldn't necessarily have had to be your job. Even if the moral norm that produced the best outcome was for some of the individuals receptive to the moral norm to volunteer to resist individually, that doesn't mean you were in that set of individuals. And besides that, there were more options for contributing to fixing the problem than just individual independence. It was a question of what economists call "comparative advantage". (Christians might refer to 1 Corinthians 12, the one about many parts and one body and differing gifts: "If the whole body were an eye, where would be the sense of hearing? If the whole body were an ear, where would be the sense of smell?")

(Separately, there are unseen phenomena affecting whether divination is reliable, and even though all the reports I see are that geomancy (and sometimes Yijing) are more robust to those phenomena than other systems, it might be best to stay hedged against the possibility that you'd trip over an exception. For instance I half remember something about Tarot sometimes giving up in questions where the querent and the quaesitor are the same person and they have a strong emotional attachment in the question, and just telling them what they want to hear, or (reading between the lines) perhaps what would create the most instructive drama... Come to think of it, I could see that as a reasonable passive-aggressive response, if I (as the oracle) had looked into the other counterfactual scenarios where I had given other judgements and the querent had misinterpreted them, although of course the passive-aggression might be lost on the querent, since they only remembered the way they interpreted the judgement I gave in the version of history that was actually realized. Sorry, that was a tangent. If geomancy itself behaves unexpectedly in some situations, I haven't heard what those situations would be, other than when the question (and therefore the querent's interpretational frame) is ill-posed and requires Gricean implicature as above. Though really all mortal questions are like that to some degree, however small.)

Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: Two timing questions
replies.content:
1) The astrological new year is at the spring equinox. Any of the equinoxes or solstices makes a good starting point, though.

2) These days I can usually place a completed manuscript within days, and tolerably often it's placed with a publisher before I finish it -- or even before I begin. The other time factors are not so brief! Depending on the book, it takes me between six months and three years to research and write the manuscript, and the publisher usually takes between one and two years to get it into print. So, yes, I always have to have an eye to the future.

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List, Happy New Year
comments.content:
PRAYER LIST UPDATE


The Ecosophia Prayer List celebrated its one year anniversay on December 22nd! In the last year, there were many more prayer requests than I originally anticipated, but I'm happy for it. Several people reported serious illness recovering faster than doctors expected, including cases which weren't expected to turn around at all.

One piece of feedback that I've gotten is that some people are overwhelmed by the sheer number of prayer entries. This is completely understandable; there are indeed many, and is a bit daunting even to me. I think some new tactics are warranted to manage the list.

First of all, as I've been warning I would do for some weeks, I've removed all old prayers older than 6 months (with a couple of exceptions that my intuition encouraged me to keep). Next, I am revising the policy regarding how long entries remain on the list:

  • Except where specifically requested otherwise, all prayers for the dead will come off of the list after a month and a day. 
  • The same goes for prayer requests for pets: a month and a day, unless specifically requested otherwise. 
  • All other prayers can remain for 3 months without an update (though updates are appreciated). This will be a harder line than I allowed before. I reserve the discretion to allow certain prayers for longer, when serious medical issues or the like are involved.
As before, updates on how things are going with what you've made a request about are always appreciated. Every time you, if your prayer is not yet resolved, the 3 month clock starts over.

As I've changed the terms, all entries that are currently remaining on the list are grandfathered in, and safe from such removal until the Spring Equinox.

May the coming year be filled with blessings for all!

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests.

Tyler A's pregnant wife Monika is at high rish for an ectopic pregnancy. May it turn out that the fetus has implanted in the right place, and may mother and child enjoy good health going forward.

May Frank Rudolf Hartman of Altadena California (picture), who is receiving chemotherapy, be completely cured of the lymphoma that is afflicting him, and may he return to full health. 

May the brain surgery that Erika's partner James underwent for his cancer on October 16th have gone successfully; and may he be blessed, healed and protected, and successfully treated for all of his cancer.

May Kyle's friend Amanda, who though in her early thirties is undergoing various difficult treatments for brain cancer, make a full recovery; and may her body and spirit heal with grace.

Lp9's hometown, East Palestine, Ohio, for the safety and welfare of their people, animals and all living beings in and around East Palestine, and to improve the natural environment there to the benefit of all.
 
* * *

Old guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ. (It does not yet accord with the new policies I've listed above; I'll update it soon.)

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. If this appeals to you, I invite you to join me.
